coredata.dc:title: Flexible biodegradable electrochemical sensor of PBAT and CNDs composite for the detection of emerging pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Maciel, Cristiane C.
de Barros, Anerise
Mazali, Italo Odone
Ferreira, Marystela
coredata.dc:description: Herein we proposed the fabrication and optimization of a flexible electrochemical sensor composed of polymer poly(butylene adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT) and Carbon Nitrite Dots (CNDs). The materials stand out for being low-cost, flexible, biodegradable, and easily discarded after use. Characterization was performed by Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), contact angle, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The sensor was used to simultaneously detect emerging pollutants such as cadmium and copper by Square Wave Anodic Stripping Voltammetry (SWV) technique. As result, a linear behavior in the concentration range of 0.1 to 1.0 mM for cadmium and copper showed excellent electrochemical activity with sensitivity of (3.79 ± 0.21) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for cadmium and (2.06 ± 0.14) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for copper. The LD and LQ values obtained for cadmium were 2.75 × 10−6 M and 9.16 × 10−6 M, and for copper were 7.09 × 10−6 M and 2.36 × 10−5 M, respectively. Therefore, the sensors obtained in this work have the advantage of simultaneously detecting emerging pollutants such as cadmium and copper, including real tap water samples.

Cyclic Voltammetry and Nyquist plots for electrodes containing CNDs (10, 15 and 20%) on PBAT matrix. (A, C) without UVB light application and (B, D) with UVB light application. Analysis parameters: CV was used in a potential range from − 0.15 V to + 0.5 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; EIS was employed in a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 1x105 Hz. All measurements were carried out in 0.1 M KCl and 5.0 × 10−3 M Fe(CN)6 −3/−4.

Cyclic voltammetry for the PBAT/CNDs electrode exposed to UVB light (A) and without exposure (B). Chronoamperometry with and without exposure of PBAT/CNDs in UVB light (C). Conditions: CV was used in a potential range from − 0.15 V to + 0.5 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; EIS was employed in a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 1x105 Hz. All measurements were carried out in 0.1 M KCl and 5.0 × 10−3 M Fe(CN)6 −3/−4.

(A) Angle analysis on the surface of the PBAT polymer and on the PBAT/CNDs composite. (B) FTIR spectra of PBAT and the PBAT/CNDs composite with and without UVB light exposure. (C) XPS spectroscopic data for high-resolution analysis for the C1, O1, and N1 peaks of the PBAT/CNDs composite. (D) XRD spectra for PBAT, CNDs, and the PBAT/CNDs composite.

Optimization parameters for square wave voltammetry electrochemical technique, frequency (A), pulse amplitude (B), preconcentration time (C), accumulation potential (D) and supporting electrolyte (E).
SWV voltammetric responses using PBAT/CNDs electrode in the simultaneous determination of cadmium and copper. The parameters used for SWV were the same as described in Table 3.

SWV profiles for detecting cadmium in isolation over a concentration range of 0.1 to 1.0 mM using PBAT/CNDs and their respective calibration curve by current density vs. analyte concentration for cadmium (A) and copper (C); detection of copper over a concentration range of 0.1 to 1.0 mM using PBAT/CNDs and their respective calibration curve by current density vs. analyte concentration for cadmium (B) and copper (D).
Zeta potential of neat PBAT film and PBAT film with CNDs.

Electrochemical parameters obtained by CV and EIS measurements for electrodes fabricated with CNDs with and without UVB light activation and the g-PBAT electrode. The geometric areas were 0.28 cm2 for both electrodes (g-PBAT and PBAT/CNDs).

head.ce:title: Flexible biodegradable electrochemical sensor of PBAT and CNDs composite for the detection of emerging pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we proposed the fabrication and optimization of a flexible electrochemical sensor composed of polymer poly(butylene adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT) and Carbon Nitrite Dots (CNDs). The materials stand out for being low-cost, flexible, biodegradable, and easily discarded after use. Characterization was performed by Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), contact angle, Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The sensor was used to simultaneously detect emerging pollutants such as cadmium and copper by Square Wave Anodic Stripping Voltammetry (SWV) technique. As result, a linear behavior in the concentration range of 0.1 to 1.0 mM for cadmium and copper showed excellent electrochemical activity with sensitivity of (3.79 ± 0.21) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for cadmium and (2.06 ± 0.14) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for copper. The LD and LQ values obtained for cadmium were 2.75 × 10−6 M and 9.16 × 10−6 M, and for copper were 7.09 × 10−6 M and 2.36 × 10−5 M, respectively. Therefore, the sensors obtained in this work have the advantage of simultaneously detecting emerging pollutants such as cadmium and copper, including real tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic metal pollution is an imminent danger worldwide, causing soil and water contamination, which leads to bioaccumulation in the ecosystem and endangers the health of humans and living beings. For instance, metals such as cadmium and copper are highly toxic even in small amounts and can cause long-term damage to the human bone and neurological system and reproductive ability and can even induce cancer [1–4]. Thus, there is great interest in detecting these pollutants, especially in water. Nowadays, conventional methods to detect these compounds use sophisticated techniques and equipment that hinders field analysis, such as chromatography and x-ray fluorescence [5–10]. In this way, the electrochemical methods have been frequently used because they have high sensitivity, easy operation, fast response, portability, and accessibility.

The advantage of working with electrochemical techniques is associated to the devices built for detection in various ways, including the possibility of miniaturization of the system, as also reported by the vast works in the literature [11–15]. Additionally, the miniaturized system facilitates its handling and reduces the volume of sample required. An essential factor in the development of system devices is associated with the materials choice that can be easily disposable. Therefore, the present work proposed the use of a 100% biodegradable fossil source material, a copolyester called poly (butylene adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT) that combines in its structure aliphatic and aromatic parts, is flexible and has good thermal and mechanical properties at room temperature [16–18].

In this case, PBAT is characterized as isolating material and used as a polymeric base for the electrochemical sensor fabrication. On the other hand, Carbon Nitrite Dots (CNDs) have semispherical morphology, of which the diameter covers the range between 1 nm and 100 nm [19–21], exhibit several interesting properties due to their small dimensions and surface area, with easy obtainability, low cost, high water solubility, luminescence, and good chemical stability [22]. In addition, CNDs can be semiconductors, due to the quantum confinement, in the three dimensions of space of the charge carriers (electron/hole pair). Nevertheless, CNDs are nanoparticles that exhibit heterogeneous structures, tunable physical, chemical, electronic, and optical properties, therefore, they offer a promising alternative for application such as biosensing, electrochemical sensing, biomedicine, bioimaging, optoelectronics, and energy conversion [20,21,23,24]. The main advantageous of CNDs in electrochemical sensors is associated their excellent conductivity properties, guaranteeing the redox process on the electrode interface during the analysis. We highlight the use of CNDs for the output of sensors [22,25,26], which are synthesized from citric acid and urea based on a simple and easy preparation process, higher surface-to-volume ratio, better chemical and thermal stability, nanoscale size, and abundant surface functional groups [20,27–29]. The combination of CNDs and polymer-based (PBAT) materials is the key to this work to obtain flexible and self-sustaining electrodes.

In this context, we developed a disposable sensor using the solvent evaporation technique and silver conductive ink to assemble a miniaturized system. Furthermore, it allowed the production of a sensor with properties such as low cost, good stability, fast response time, low detection limit and good selectivity when applied to simultaneous electrochemical detection of cadmium and copper.

All reagents used in this work were of analytical grade. BASF supplied poly (butylene adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT) under the trade name Ecoflex® F Blend C1200. Other materials such as urea, citric acid, potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium chloride (KCl), monobasic potassium phosphate (KH2PO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. cadmium chloride (CdCl2·H2O) and copper (II) chloride (CuCl2) were obtained commercially from Vetec. Ag/AgCl conductive ink was purchased from LabSolution. Chloroform (GC grade) and dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Synth. All working solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (Sartorius Weighing Technology, Germany) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C. For Zeta potential analysis the solutions were prepared in pH 3 and 7, while for the electrochemistry study a range pH between 2 and 6 was used, both in PBS buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CNDs and fabrication of the flexible and biodegradable with CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs were synthesized according to the procedure described by Liyanage et al., 2019 [22] with some modifications. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid and urea were weighed and transferred to a hydrothermal flask with 25 mL of water. This mixture was stirred vigorously for 24 h; subsequently, it was heated in the microwave at 700 W power for 5 min, then the obtained solid was washed, centrifuged, and filtered through a 0.2 µm filter, the material resultant was dried in an oven at 90 °C.

The films of PBAT and CNDs were obtained in different proportions (10, 15 and 20 % m/m of CNDs), whose steps consisted in the solubilization of CNDs in DMF in ultrasound for 1 h (Fig. 1 A) and in parallel the solubilization of the polymer in chloroform under stirring for 45 min (Fig. 1B). Next, the solubilized materials were mixed and stirred vigorously for 10 min and then placed in the ultrasonic bath for 1 h (Fig. 1C). Then the film was poured into a petri dish and dried in an oven at 40 °C; after the film was dry and cut for sensor assembly (Fig. 1D).

The base material used to assemble the reference, working and counter electrodes were based on PBAT and graphite (g-PBAT) as described by Maciel et al., 2022 [18]. However, the g-PBAT electrode was modified with Ag/AgCl ink to be used as the quasi - reference electrode and modified with a PBAT-CNDs film with a thickness of 1.0 mm to be used as the working electrode. And the counter electrode was the g-PBAT itself.

The CNDs were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy in a Shimadzu RF5301pc equipment, employing λex = 340 nm and emission scan from 350 to 600 nm. Morphological characterization of the CNDs was performed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), JEOL 2100F model TEM-Bright Field-imaging, spot sizes of 1 nm, and camera length 10 cm. The sample was dripped onto a model C FlatTM grid holder located at the National Nanotechnology Laboratory (LNNano). The wettability of the composite substrate was measured by the sessile drop technique in a Ramé Hart goniometer (model 100–00) with deionized water. Structural characterization was performed using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) Nicolet Summit IR 200 FT-IR using 120 scans, with a resolution of 4.0 cm−1, in the range 500 to 4000 cm−1. A HORIBA Jobin Yvon T64000 Raman Spectrometer coupled with an Olympus BX41 microscope was used to perform the measurements, with an incident excitation radiation of wavelength equal to 633 nm from a He-Ne laser from Research Electro-Optics, available at UNICAMP - Institute of Chemistry. For XPS analysis, an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, model K-Alpha, from Thermo Scientific, located at the National Nanotechnology Laboratory (LNNano), was used. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed in a Rigaku diffractometer (RU200B) with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm), a step of 0.0200/sec and speed of 1°/min using voltage and generator current of 40 kV, and 60 mA. The zeta potential analyses of the films were conducted in an electrokinetic analyzer (SurPASS 3, Anton Paar, GmbH, Austria). KCl (0.1 M) was used as electrolyte, at room temperature, at pH 3 and 7, under the film surface (neat PBAT and PBAT/CNDs films).

The entire electrochemical system was placed correctly in a wooden box covered with a thermal aluminum blanket, whose dimensions are 30x17x12 cm (length × width × height). In the upper part of the box, a UVB lamp 15 W was attached so that the entire electrochemical analysis could be performed with and without the application of light, the scheme is represented in Figure SI 1.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out using a potentiostat/galvanostatic model PGSTAT30 from Autolab (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, Netherlands). The electrochemical characterizations were carried out in the presence of 5 × 10−3 M Fe(CN)6 −3/−4 and 0.1 M KCl by CV (scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and potential range from −0,15 V to 0.5 V) and EIS (frequency ranging from 1x105 Hz to 0.1 Hz and 10 mV of amplitude).

The SWV technique was used to detect toxic metals (cadmium and copper) simultaneously, and the pH study was performed over a potential range of −0.9 V to + 0.2 V with an amplitude of 100 mV and with a preconcentration time of 95 s. The same conditions were used to perform the analytical curve and real sample analyses. The supporting electrode solution consisted of 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution prepared at pH 3. Furthermore, to verify the sensor selectivity we used some inorganic ions such as Fe3+, NO3– and SO4− and some organic molecules such as citric acid, nitric acid, and urea (0.5 mM in PBS solution) as potential interfering species was verified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNDs synthesis and PBAT/CNDs composite characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: CNDs were synthesized and obtained from a straightforward, short-duration, and pollution-free process. The effective production of CNDs was characterized by the fluorescence spectrum from the emission band around 450 nm when excited in 340 nm, as can be seen in Figure SI 2(A). This result follows the literature, whose band shows a full width at half maxima (FHWM) of 506, 253, and 104, indicating that the lower values for FWHM are associated with the control over the surface states or emission centers of the CNDs [22,30]. Additionally, TEM micrography was obtained for CNDs to verify the morphology and size distribution. It is possible to observe in Figure SI 2(B) that the CNDs present a shape of the particles closer to spheres. From this, we determine their size distribution by histogram an average size of 78.38 ± 22.01 nm, indicating a good dispersion for the CNDs obtained, as can be seen in Figure SI 2(C).

In order to verify the influence CNDs on the PBAT polymer matrix, we previously investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) techniques under UVB light and without UVB light conditions. For this, a study was performed by using 10, 15 and 20% of CNDs on PBAT (w/w), using a Fe(CN)6 −3/−4 solution (5.0 × 10−3 M) as redox probe. Fig. 2 (A, C) shows the CV and EIS characterization without UVB light, whose electrode with 10% CNDs showed a higher current density (0.436 ± 0.029 (mA/cm2)) and a lower Rct value (30.49 ± 1.86 (KΩ cm2)), compared to the electrodes with 15% and 20% CNDs. A similar behavior was observed when UVB light was applied (Fig. 2 (B, D)), i.e., an increase in current density (0.592 ± 0.017 (mA/cm2)) and lower values for Rct (21.61 ± 1.23 (KΩ cm2)) for the sample containing 10% of CNDs is observed by comparison to the samples containing 15 and 20%. Thus, the electrodes composed with 10% CNDs in the presence of UVB light incidence provide an increase of 0.156 mA/cm2 in current density compared to the electrode without light application. These results reveal that the UVB light incidence allows the faster activation of electrons from the sensor [23,31]. On the other hand, a higher percentage of CNDs (15 and 20%) on PBAT matrix leads to a decrease in current density, consequently, an increase in the Rct value. This fact can be related to the agglomeration of CNDs on PBAT matrix, whose surface area is eventually decreased and, consequently, the electrochemical activity is also reduced, causing a higher resistance to charge transfer. Therefore, these results were essential to defining the ratio of CNDs on the PBAT matrix (PBAT/CNDs), guaranteeing a better electrochemical response for further applications.

In addition, we also verified the effect of UVB light application in the PBAT/CNDs sensor as in the function of time exposed by CV and chronoamperometry. Fig. 3 (A) shows the CV performed on the PBAT/CNDs sensor with exposure to UVB light over a period of ten minutes, corresponding to the ten subsequent measurements. The CV results present an increase of 36% for the current intensity until the fourth measurement, after that, a decrease in the current intensity is observed. This fact is related to the loss in fluorescence intensity caused by the own light, and we confirmed by CV measurements without UVB light application, as shown in Fig. 3(B). However, the maximum current intensity reached (Fig. 3(B)) corresponds to only 23% of the minimum current compared to the CV exposed to UVB light in Fig. 3(A). Corroborated with these results, the chronoamperometry of PBAT/CNDs showed in Fig. 3(C) with and without the presence of UVB light reveals that the current gradually decreased for an interval of 1200 s, proving once again the loss of fluorescence intensity in the sensor when exposed to light. The PBAT/CNDs sensor can be used for metal ion detection because in the presence of the UVB light application does not interfere of the electrochemical response, unlike organic molecules that can be degraded during the light exposure [32–34].

Based on the previous results obtained by CV and EIS for the PBAT/CNDs containing 10% of CNDs, we also characterized the PBAT/CNDs sensors properties from the contact angle, FTIR, XPS, XRD, and zeta potential. The combination of CNDs and PBAT leads to a decrease in the contact angle value compared to neat PBAT, i.e., 89.5° ± 1.8° for PBAT and 60.1° ± 0.2° for PABT/CNDs sensor, as can be seen in Fig. 4 (A). The change in contact angle value indicates an increase in the wettability of the electrode surface provided by the presence of CNDs in PBAT matrix, and also suggests a decrease in the cohesive force, which seeks to contain the spreading of droplets on a surface. This result shows that the insertion of the CNDs into the polymer matrix slightly modified its surface, allowing it to become more hydrophilic compared to the neat PBAT. Specifically, the surface wettability contributes to a better interaction of the analyte detection in aqueous media and the electrode interface.

To better understand the functional groups of the CNDs modification on the PBAT polymer surface with and without UVB light exposure FTIR characterization was performed. Fig. 4(B) shows the spectrum of neat PBAT and the composite PBAT/ CNDs, we divided the spectra in two parts for discussion, i.e., part 1 corresponds to the spectral range of 4000 – 2500 cm−1, while part 2 corresponds to the 2500 – 500 cm−1. Observed in the spectra of part 1 (Fig. 4(B)) the presence of three weak bands at 3202, 3059, and 2759 cm−1 for the PBAT/CNDs unidentified for neat PBAT spectrum. More specifically, the presence of bending vibrations of the CN group was confirmed by the band at 3202 cm−1, and the band at 3058 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the NH group, admitting the availability of these groups on the surface of the PBAT/CNDs composite. The band at 2759 cm−1 is attributed to CN binding, indicating the existence of amino-containing functional groups [34,35]. On the other hand, in part 2 (Fig. 4(B)) we observed only relative intensity changes for the spectrum of PBAT/CNDs in comparison to the neat PBAT spectrum. Table SI 1 in the supplementary material includes the assignment band details. Therefore, the FTIR spectra confirmed the effective interaction of CNDs and PBAT polymeric matrix. Corroborated with the FTIR data, the XPS spectra were obtained through three dominant high-resolution peaks assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s (Fig. 4C), suggesting the existence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum showed for the composite a “shake-up” signal at 292.84 eV due to the π → π*-type transition [36]. In addition, it showed CC structure peak at 280.29 eV and O–CO peak at 290.32 eV attributed to the polymer structure. The high-resolution N1 spectrum, it was possible to show the CNH bonding signal at 399.41 eV and at 401.22 the NH group for the PBAT/CNDs composite. The region of oxygenated groups can be seen in the high-resolution O1 spectrum, the PBAT/CNDs composite showed a 531.67 eV peak of the CO structure, and at 533.44 eV it showed the O–CO group, corresponding to ester bonds [37,38]. Table SI 2 details the peaks found with their respective atomic percentages.

The presence of CNDs in PBAT polymeric matrix was also elucidated by XRD characterization, as can be seen in Fig. 4(D). More specifically, the neat CNDs possess a single broad peak centered at about 27° (2θ) corresponding to the crystal lattice distance of (002) (d002 = 0.34 nm), indicating of CNDs is predominantly amorphous, and possess a highly disordered character of the carbon structure with an interplanar spacing of 3.55 Å. This result follows the results shown in the literature [39–41]. On the other hand, the neat PBAT diffractogram (Fig. 4 (D)) is also characterized by the peaks at 16, 17, 20, 23, and 25° (2θ), indicating a semi-crystalline structure [42]. The broadening observed in the PBAT diffractogram in the region of these peaks is associated with the amorphous phase of the PBAT, characteristic of polymeric material. Finally, the PBAT/CNDs diffractogram showed both peaks characteristics of materials, whose characteristics peaks of PBAT appear at 17, 18, 21, 24, and 26° (2θ), while of CNDs appear at 31° (2θ). A slight shift (1° (2θ)) is observed on the PBAT characteristics peaks in the presence of CNDs, while for CNDs a shifting peak of 4°(2 θ) is identified for the PBAT/CNDs composite. We suggested that this shifting can be associated with the high dispersity of CNDs on the PBAT polymeric matrix. This result is in accordance with our Raman spectra (results not shown), where the bands of PBAT are predominant.

The potential zeta for neat PBAT and PBAT/CNDs was evaluated to verify the sensitivity of composite in acid pH, since the metal pollutants are susceptible to more acidic pHs. The analysis was performed on the substrate itself, i.e., the equipment performs the measurement on the solid surface of the film, in this case, the neat PBAT film and PBAT/CNDs [43]. For this, we assessed using an acidic pH (3.0) and a neutral pH (7.0), as presented in Table 1 . The results indicate the negative zeta potential for both films (neat PBAT and PBAT/CNDs) for different pHs (3.0 and 7.0). More specifically, the neat PBAT film has a potential zeta of −12.01 and −28.04 mV, for pHs 3 and 7, respectively. While PBAT/CNDs film has zeta potential of −52.75 and −49.64 mV, for pHs 3 and 7, respectively. We can claim that inserting CNDs on the PBAT polymeric matrix favors a strong negative potential. Furthermore, our sensors are highly resistant to the acid pH, favoring the metal pollutants susceptible to more acidic pHs and not precipitating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization and parameters optimization for PBAT/CNDs sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
For the system of three electrodes proposed in this work, we used as counter electrode graphite and PBAT (g-PBAT) polymeric matrix, as shown in Fig. 1. For this, a previous characterization and parameters optimization for g-PBAT and PBAT/CNDs in the presence and absence of UVB light used as electrochemical sensors were evaluated by CV and EIS by using [Fe(CN)6]−3/4 in 0.1 M KCl (5 × 10−3 M). The study of current density versus different scan rate is shown in Figure SI 3 by plots of cathodic/anodic peak. From this, we used the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (1), to obtain parameters such as electroactive area (A) (cm2), resistance charge transfer (Rct), and heterogeneous electron transfer constant (k0). Where Ip is the peak current, n is the number of electrons transferred, D is the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]−3/4 in a 0.1 M KCl solution (7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), C is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]−3/4 (mol cm−3), and v is the scan rate (V s−1).

As described in Table 2 , the corresponding electroactive area of PBAT/CNDs with UVB light application showed an increase of 64.43% compared to the sensor without exposed light. An increase of 85.84% could also be seen compared to the g-PBAT electrode. In addition, the electrochemically active area of the PBAT/CNDs sensor with UVB light application showed a 19-fold increase compared to its geometric area, which is around 0.28 cm2.

From the Nyquist plot obtained by EIS it was possible to find the Rct values for each sensor (Table 2). The results showed a lower Rct for the PBAT/CNDs sensor exposure to UVB light (18.33 KΩ cm2) compared to the electrode without light application (29.50 KΩ cm2). However, regardless of the UVB light, the sensors fabricated with the CNDs showed an increase in Rct values compared to the sensor fabricated with graphite, which has an Rct value of 15.31 ± 1.18 KΩ cm2, as can be seen in Table 2. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the amount of carbon material used to make the sensors are very different, i.e., while using 10% CNDs to produce the PBAT/CNDs sensor, it is necessary to use 233% graphite to produce the g-PBAT sensor, in a mass-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the electron transfer rate constant (k0) was determined from Eq. (2):

Where R is the molar constant of the gas (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T the thermodynamic temperature (298 K), F the Faraday constant (96485C mol−1), A the electroactive area of the electrode (Table 2), and C the concentration of electroactive species (5 × 10−6 mol cm−3). As expected, the k0 value obtained for the g-PBAT sensor unit was significantly higher compared to the sensor with CNDs, indicating higher electrochemical activity. From the results discussed for the sensor produced with PBAT and CNDs, all subsequent electrochemical measurements of this work will be with the application of UVB light.

From the parameters optimized for g-PBAT and PBAT/CNDs previously discussed, the SWV parameters were optimized using the target analyte (cadmium and copper ions, Cd2+ and Cu2+). All optimization measurements were acquired in 0.1 M PBS buffer, pH 3.0, with 20 µL (0.1 M) of Cd2+ and Cu2+ as following the sequence: frequency, amplitude, time, accumulation potential, and supporting electrolyte. For the frequency, we varied in a range of 10–90 Hz, which the highest current (I) being for the 90 Hz frequency with values of 0.145 and 0.104 (mA), for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively. However, in the presence of Cu2+ ions we observed a shift in the anodic peak, moving to more positive potentials for the lower frequencies, as can be seen in Fig. 5 (A). Moreover, the current intensity is also lower than the higher frequencies, and the better frequency is defined at 90 Hz. The second parameter evaluated was the pulse amplitude, analyzed by the 15–100 mV range. In this case, the increase in the amplitude values, both Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions, are slightly shifted anodic peak is observed to positive potentials. Even with a slight shifting for higher amplitude values, 100 mV was chosen due to high current intensity, which showed a current equal to 6.0 an 3.92 (mA) for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively (Fig. 5(B)).

The effect of preconcentration time was the third parameter evaluated, using a 15–120 s range, as presented in Fig. 5(C). The anodic peak current of the metals increased discretely as the preconcentration time was increased, for which the time of 95 s was chosen, showing 7.58 (mA) for cadmium and 4.19 (mA) for copper. The accumulation potential was optimized for −1.5–1.0 V, where a better result was observed for −1.3 to −0.9 V, as seen in Fig. 5(D). As it is known, the simultaneous determination of different metals can vary as the accumulation potential changes, so this parameter was evaluated to verify the best performance for the two metals. For instance, Wei et al. 2011 [44], investigated the response for Cd2+ and Cu2+ using an rGO/SnO2 nanocomposite by varying the potential from 0 to −1.4 V, reaching a maximum current peak when the potential of −1.0 V was applied, for both metals. The current values reached the ideal electrochemical response for the accumulation potential of −1.1 V, with Cd2+ showing 8.05 (mA) and 5.88 (mA) for Cu2+. However, the maximum current intensity response for Cu2+ ions was an accumulation potential of −1.3 V, dramatically decreasing the current intensity value for Cd2+ detection, as seen in Fig. 5(D).

Finally, the supporting electrolyte influence is crucial for improving analytical characteristics, such as LD and LQ of the target analyte. This factor is directly related to the electron transfer kinetics of the modified electrode. For this, several studies reported in the literature has been used acetate buffer or phosphate buffer as a supporting electrolyte in the analysis of heavy metals, including the simultaneous determination of cadmium, lead, and copper ions in drinking water [2,5,45]. Based on this, we evaluated as supporting electrolyte, HCl, KCl, and phosphate buffer (all in pH 3.0) using the previous parameters defined, and the SWV for this characterization can be seen in Fig. 5(E). Note that the Cd2+ and Cu2+ peaks detection are better represented when using the phosphate buffer (PBS) as a supporting electrolyte, as well as a higher current intensity is observed. In addition, this supporting electrolyte (PBS) is very interesting for our analysis since it allows greater control of pH. Table 3 briefly presents the parameters chosen to be used in subsequent experiments. Additionally, the pH effect was also evaluated for 0.1 M PBS solution varying the pH value from 2.0 to 6.0, as shown in Figure SI 4. As expected, the pH 3.0 presented the better result, with current density value of 3.77 (mA/cm2) for Cd2+ and 1.94 (mA/cm2) for Cu2+.

Therefore, considering the parameters optimized above, we characterized the metal ions from individual and simultaneous detection from SWV measurements using different concentrations. The analysis was performed using a PBAT/CNDs electrode in the presence of UVB light, and the calibration curve was constructed over a concentration range of 0.1 to 1.0 mM for Cd2+ and Cu2+ in PBS buffer pH 3.0. The peaks obtained for Cu2+ ions are significantly smaller than Cd2+ since the additions were equal for both. However, similar results for detecting these metals were also found in the literature [1,2,4].

The simultaneous calibration curve of the analytes showed a potential of approximately −0.70 V for cadmium and −0.02 V for copper, Fig. 6 . The current density increased linearly for the two metals, which showed a linear correlation of R2 = 0.988 and sensitivity of (3.79 ± 0.21) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for cadmium and R2 = 0.975 and sensitivity of (2.06 ± 0.14) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for copper. The limit of detection (LD) and limit of quantification (LQ) was calculated in accordance with Miller and Miller [46] through the equation
LD =
y 0
-
y B
+ 3
S
y / x
and
LQ = 3.33 x L D
, where
y 0
-
y B
is the interception value after subtraction of the blank and
S
y / x
corresponds to the errors in the y-direction and the LQ = 3.33 × LD. The LD and LQ values obtained for cadmium were 2.75 × 10−6 M and 9.16 × 10−6 M, and for copper were 7.09 × 10−6 M and 2.36 × 10−5 M, respectively. These values indicated the success of the PBAT/CNDs sensor in detecting the metals simultaneously at low concentrations. According to the toxicological information sheet from CETESB (Companhia Ambiental do Estado de São Paulo), the allowed value in tap water (brackish) for cadmium is 45.0 × 10−6 M and for copper is 78.0 × 10−6 M [47,48], i.e., the results obtained for the LQ in the simultaneous detection of these metals were lower than the values allowed by the legislation, this means that the fabricated sensor detected the metals with only 20.35% (cadmium) and 30.25% (copper) of the allowed value.

In addition, the individual detection of metal ions Cd2+ and Cu2+ was performed to verify the changes in the voltammograms profile compared to simultaneous detections by using the same analysis conditions in the simultaneous detection. Fig. 7 (A, B) shows the calibration curves for individual determination of cadmium, which presented a relative deviation to more positive potentials with the addition of the metal, since this behavior did not occur in the simultaneous detection curve. The cadmium calibration curve showed a linear correlation of R2 = 0.997 and a sensitivity of (1.99 ± 0.36) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 with LD and LQ values of 4.86 × 10−6 M and 1.62 × 10−5 M, respectively. For the detection of copper, Fig. 7(C, D), the same phenomenon occurred concerning the potential, as the current density increased, the potential shifted to more positive potentials, differently from the behavior in the simultaneous detection. The individual copper determination exhibited a linear correlation of R2 = 0.995 and sensitivity of (2.45 ± 0.53) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 with LD and LQ values of 4.93 × 10−6 M and 1.64 × 10−5 M, respectively. The analytical performance of PBAT/CNDs was compared with other electrochemical sensors in the literature that detected cadmium and copper, Table 4 . In agreement with the LODs obtained by other sensor types produced by several authors, the PBAT/CNDs sensor proved to be within the detection range for cadmium and copper. In addition, it is worth noting that the fabrication of this electrode requires a minimal number of materials and can be easily compared with other electrodes in the literature, presenting satisfactory results for electrochemical detection.

In order to verify the reproducibility (intra-day) and repeatability (inter-day) study was performed using copper and cadmium at a concentration of 0.5 mM in 0.1 M PBS buffer. The reproducibility was obtained by employing ten different electrodes, resulting in a relative standard deviation (RSD) equal to 4.22% and 4.15% for cadmium and copper, respectively. Repeatability testing was performed over six days (inter-day) and resulted in an RSD = 4.76% for cadmium and 4.82% for copper, revealing that sensitivity showed minimal loss.

The stability of the PBAT/CNDs electrode was made from an electrode used to detect cadmium and copper and, which was subsequently stored in a container without light contact at room temperature for fifteen days. Subsequently, this electrode was subjected to the same type of detection and under the same conditions as previously used, and the peak current signals decreased by only 8.2% and 7.5% compared to the initial copper and cadmium peaks. From this, it was possible to verify that the electrochemical behavior of the sensor has good stability, moreover, no significant change was observed in the peak densities (J) of copper and cadmium. Selectivity interference evaluation is an effective way to verify the selectivity of the produced sensor. From this, we know that water sources usually contain many contaminants, such as inorganic ions Fe3+, NO3– and SO4 − [2,55]. Besides of organic molecules such as, citric acid, nitric acid and urea [56,57]. In this way, we evaluated the performance of sensors in the presence of these interferents at a concentration of 0.5 mM, including cadmium and copper at 0.5 mM concentration, in 0.1 mM PBS buffer. The results indicated that neither the potential nor the anodic peak current corresponding to copper and cadmium did not change, confirming that no interference occurred (Figure SI 5). Thus, the PBAT/CNDs showed good selectivity for the simultaneous detection of cadmium and copper in the interferents used, however, the sensor can also be used in other matrices, provided there is a study to analyze the behavior in this sensor by changing, pH, electrolyte, temperature, etc.

Finally, the real sample evaluation of the proposed electrode was performed using the addition-recovery methodology, where known amounts of cadmium and copper were added to tap water samples, and the measurements were performed using the SWV electrochemical technique under previously optimized conditions. Table 5 shows actual concentration values, concentrations found, and recovery values obtained for tap water samples. The recoveries ranged from 89.57 to 112.27% for cadmium and 81.28 to 100.76% for copper, and these results indicated that the proposed sensor showed good sensitivity for application in real samples for routine analysis of these pollutants.

The properties of the composite produced from PBAT polymer, and the addition of the nanomaterials CNDs were successfully characterized by qualitative techniques such as FTIR and XPS, which showed excellent incorporation of the CNDs in PBAT. The synthesis of CNDs was also evaluated by MET microscopy, proving the obtainment of these nanomaterials. The electrochemical experiments confirmed the best performance by CV and EIS with the exposure UVB light on the sensor. Moreover, the values obtained in the electrochemical measurements were not significantly better compared to the g-PBAT (graphite) sensor, but it was taken into account the amount of material used for the manufacture of the sensor, since for PBAT/CNDs the amounts are significantly small compared to g-PBAT.

Under optimized conditions, the proposed sensor was applied to the simultaneous determination of cadmium and copper ions by SWV. It showed excellent electrochemical activity, exhibiting a linear behavior in the concentration range of 0.1 to 1.0 mM. The metals presented a linear correlation of R2 = 0.988 and sensitivity of (3.79 ± 0.21) × 10−5 (mAcm−2) (M)−1 for cadmium and R2 = 0.975 and sensitivity of (2.06 ± 0.14) × 10−5 (mA cm−2) (M)−1 for copper. The LD and LQ values obtained for cadmium were 2.75 × 10−6 M and 9.16 × 10−6 M, and for copper were 7.09 × 10−6 M and 2.36 × 10−5 M, respectively. These values indicated the success of the PBAT/CNDs sensor in detecting the metals simultaneously at low concentrations, illustrating a simple, inexpensive, and easy preparation methodology as a viable alternative to conventional approaches for the detection of emerging pollutants.
